lessons.title: The Fractional CMO Operating Model
lessons.title_es: El modelo operativo del CMO fraccionado
lessons.description: Define scope, cadence, and deliverables for a fractional engagement that drives founder confidence.
lessons.description_es: Define el alcance, la cadencia y los entregables para un compromiso fraccionado que genere confianza en el fundador.
lessons.content:
The Fractional CMO Engagement Blueprint

A fractional CMO is not a consultant who sends strategy decks. The role is an embedded operator — part strategist, part execution director — who functions as the marketing department's most senior decision-maker on a part-time basis. Founders hire fractional CMOs because they need senior marketing leadership without the $250K-$400K annual commitment of a full-time hire. Your job is to deliver the output of a full-time CMO in 2-3 days per week.

Engagement Structure:

Week 1 — Diagnostic Sprint: Audit the existing marketing stack, team, channels, and metrics. Conduct stakeholder interviews with sales, ops, and product teams. Review the last 12 months of campaign and revenue data. Deliver a written diagnostic: current state, priority gaps, and 90-day plan. This document is the foundation of the relationship — it demonstrates strategic depth immediately.

Ongoing Operating Cadence:

  • Daily (async): Slack check-in with marketing team. Review overnight metrics. Approve/reject creative and copy.
  • Weekly: 60-minute strategy meeting with founder. 30-minute team standup. Campaign performance review.
  • Monthly: Board-level marketing report. Budget reconciliation. 30-day look-ahead with priorities.
  • Quarterly: Full strategy refresh. OKR review. Channel mix rebalance based on performance data.

Deliverable Standards: Every week, the founder should receive: (1) a written week-in-review (5 bullets maximum), (2) the top metric dashboard, and (3) next week's priority decisions that need founder input. Predictable output builds trust faster than impressive-sounding strategies.

Scope Management: Define in writing what is in and out of scope. Classic scope creep: founders add PR, investor decks, and product naming to the marketing scope. Each expansion should trigger a contract addendum. The fractional model only works when scope is bounded.

  • Never take a fractional engagement without a 90-day minimum commitment — shorter engagements don't produce measurable results
  • Require read access to ad accounts, CRM, and analytics on day one — you cannot lead what you cannot see
  • Build a shared marketing calendar with the sales team — revenue alignment is the fractional CMO's primary differentiator from a traditional agency
  • Document all strategic decisions and rationale in a shared knowledge base — this protects you and the client if leadership changes

lessons.title: Revenue Forecasting for Marketing Leaders
lessons.title_es: Pronóstico de ingresos para líderes de marketing
lessons.description: Build a bottom-up marketing forecast that connects channel activity to pipeline and closed revenue.
lessons.description_es: Construye un pronóstico de marketing de abajo hacia arriba que conecte la actividad del canal con el pipeline y los ingresos cerrados.
lessons.content:
The Bottom-Up Marketing Forecast Model

Most marketing leaders forecast by extrapolation — last quarter was $X, next quarter will be $X × 1.2. This approach is politically useful and analytically useless. The bottom-up forecast model builds revenue projections from channel-level activity data, creating a causal map between marketing inputs and revenue outputs.

The Forecast Model Structure:

Start with your conversion funnel metrics for each channel, measured over the last 90 days:

  • Channel → Visitor volume
  • Visitor → Lead conversion rate
  • Lead → Sales-qualified lead (SQL) rate
  • SQL → Closed-won rate
  • Average deal value

From these five numbers, you can calculate: Revenue per visitor = Visit-to-lead rate × Lead-to-SQL rate × Close rate × ACV. This is your channel's revenue productivity coefficient.

Building the Forecast: For each channel (paid search, organic, email, social, referral), apply the revenue productivity coefficient to your planned visitor volume for the period. Sum across channels to get total projected revenue. Compare to the revenue target. If there's a gap, identify which channel investments are most efficient at closing it.

The Sensitivity Table: Build a table showing revenue outcomes at different conversion rate assumptions (−20%, baseline, +20%) and at different traffic volume assumptions. This turns a single-point forecast into a range with upside and downside scenarios. Boards and founders respond better to ranges with clear drivers than to single-number forecasts.

Forecast Review Cadence: Reforecast monthly. Update actuals vs. projected. Identify channels that are over or underperforming projections. Diagnose the cause before adjusting the budget — a channel underperforming on leads may have a close rate problem in sales, not a lead quality problem in marketing.

  • Separate new customer revenue from expansion revenue in the forecast — they have different marketing cost structures
  • Track forecast accuracy as a metric — a CMO who consistently hits within 15% of forecast builds enormous credibility
  • Include marketing investment (budget) in the forecast model and calculate projected marketing ROI per scenario
  • Always build the forecast in a shared spreadsheet, not a presentation — founders and CFOs will want to manipulate the assumptions themselves

lessons.title: Building and Managing a Marketing Team
lessons.title_es: Construir y gestionar un equipo de marketing
lessons.description: Hire, structure, and lead a lean marketing team that outperforms larger, slower competitors.
lessons.description_es: Contrata, estructura y lidera un equipo de marketing compacto que supere a competidores más grandes y lentos.
lessons.content:
The Lean Marketing Team Blueprint

The most effective growth-stage marketing teams have 3-6 people and outperform enterprise marketing departments 10x their size. They do this through role clarity, tight feedback loops, and a bias toward execution over meetings. Here's how to build and run one.

The Core Roles (in hiring priority order):

1. Demand Generation Manager: Owns paid acquisition and lead volume. Must be fluent in at least two paid channels (Meta, Google, LinkedIn). This is your first hire — without demand, everything else is irrelevant. Hire someone who has managed $50K+/month in ad spend independently.

2. Content / SEO Strategist: Owns organic traffic growth, blog, and social content calendar. Should understand keyword strategy, internal linking, and content distribution. This role compounds — their work gets more valuable every month as domain authority grows.

3. Marketing Operations: Owns the CRM, automation, lead scoring, and attribution. The unsexy role that makes everything else measurable. Without someone who owns data hygiene and workflow automation, you're managing a black box.

4. Designer / Creative: Owns visual output across all channels. At growth stage, this is often a contractor. Set clear brand guidelines and a self-service asset library so other team members don't bottleneck on design.

Team Operating System:

  • Weekly standup (30 min max): Each person states: done last week, doing this week, blockers. No status reports in the meeting — those are async.
  • Monthly OKR check-in: Are we on track? What's off track and why? What do we need to change?
  • Quarterly planning (half day): Review last quarter results, set next quarter OKRs, allocate budget.

Hiring Red Flags: Candidates who can't speak to specific results with numbers. Candidates who blame previous employers for failures. Candidates who've never managed a budget. At growth stage, you need operators, not theorists.

  • Use a paid test project ($200-500) before hiring — it filters for execution ability better than any interview
  • Document all recurring tasks in a runbook — team knowledge should live in systems, not people
  • Measure team members on outcomes (leads, pipeline, revenue), not activities (posts published, emails sent)
  • Review capacity quarterly — a team member who's busy on low-impact work is an invisible budget leak

lessons.title: The 90-Day Marketing Plan: From Audit to Revenue
lessons.title_es: El plan de marketing de 90 días: del diagnóstico a los ingresos
lessons.description: Build a repeatable 90-day planning process that keeps marketing aligned with business goals.
lessons.description_es: Construye un proceso de planificación de 90 días repetible que mantenga el marketing alineado con los objetivos del negocio.
lessons.content:
The 90-Day Marketing Operating Cycle

Ninety days is the ideal planning horizon for growth-stage marketing: short enough to respond to market changes, long enough to see campaign results. The companies that execute 90-day cycles consistently outperform those with annual plans because they compound learning faster.

Phase 1 — Days 1-14: Diagnostic and Alignment

Conduct a full channel audit: Which channels are producing qualified pipeline? What is the cost per lead and cost per acquisition by channel? What is the current conversion rate at each funnel stage? Interview sales leadership: What objections kill deals? What do the best customers have in common? This phase ends with a written brief: three priority marketing problems and three 90-day hypotheses for solving them.

Phase 2 — Days 15-60: Execution Sprint

Launch experiments against each hypothesis. Keep each experiment small and measurable. For paid channels: minimum $1,500/week per channel for 30 days to generate statistically meaningful data. For content: 8-10 pieces within a topic cluster to test topical resonance. For email: a 4-email sequence with a minimum 1,000-person list segment. Run at least one experiment per hypothesis simultaneously — do not run sequentially.

Phase 3 — Days 61-90: Optimize and Double Down

Kill the experiments that aren't working. Double the budget on the experiments showing positive ROAS or lead quality. Begin building repeatable systems around the winners: SOPs, automated workflows, and content templates. Prepare the next 90-day plan based on validated learnings.

The 90-Day Report Card: At day 90, deliver a written report with: (1) original hypotheses vs. results, (2) what you'd do differently, (3) next quarter priorities and budget recommendation. This document is the most important deliverable of the engagement — it proves ROI and justifies renewal.

  • Set only 3-5 OKRs per 90-day cycle — more creates diffusion of effort and unclear accountability
  • Block 2 hours per week for strategic review — reactive CMOs lose the strategic altitude that justifies their fee
  • Create a shared 90-day calendar visible to all stakeholders — it prevents scope creep and manages expectations
  • Always have a "next cycle" conversation at day 75, not day 90 — last-minute renewals signal lack of demonstrated value
